edges.description: Loss of GALT activity causes intracellular accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate.
edges.source: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

edges.description: GALT deficiency also contributes to depletion of intracellular UDP-hexose pools.
edges.source: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

edges.description: Deficiency of the transferase step can shunt galactose metabolism toward galactitol accumulation.
edges.source: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

edges.description: Untreated classic GALT-related galactosemia produces acute neonatal hepatocellular injury.
edges.source: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

edges.description: Impaired galactose metabolism can produce persistent brain dysfunction despite galactose restriction.
edges.source: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

edges.description: Classic galactosemia is associated with diminished bone mineral content through incompletely resolved long-term mechanisms.
edges.source: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

edges.description: Reduced galactokinase activity permits excess galactose conversion to galactitol in the lens.
Attenuated GALE activity in epimerase-deficiency galactosemia is associated with elevated blood galactose-1-phosphate.
Accumulated galactose metabolites contribute to downstream endoplasmic reticulum stress.
Accumulated galactose metabolites contribute to downstream signaling abnormalities.
Accumulated galactose metabolites contribute to downstream oxidative stress.
Depleted UDP-hexose pools impair downstream glycoprotein galactosylation.
Abnormal galactosylation is one proposed contributor to chronic cognitive impairment.
Disordered galactosylation is one proposed contributor to persistent speech impairment.
Impaired glycosylation is one proposed contributor to ovarian dysfunction in classic galactosemia.
Lens galactitol accumulation drives cataract formation.
Acute hepatocellular dysfunction contributes to neonatal jaundice.
Acute liver injury contributes to hepatomegaly in untreated neonates.
Acute metabolic and hepatic dysfunction contributes to poor early feeding and growth.
Untreated neonatal metabolic decompensation includes high risk of E. coli sepsis.
Chronic neurologic involvement includes adult motor dysfunction with tremor as a frequent finding.

edges.source: Calcium, vitamin K1, and vitamin D3 supplementation

edges.description: Elevated erythrocyte Gal-1-P reports the core intracellular Gal-1-P accumulation caused by the Leloir-pathway block.
Absent or barely detectable erythrocyte GALT activity directly reports the GALT enzymatic deficiency.
edges.source: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

edges.description: Elevated urinary galactitol reports diversion of galactose to galactitol accumulation.

edges.target: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

nodes.description: Untreated neonatal classic galactosemia produces acute hepatocellular injury with liver dysfunction, feeding intolerance, and systemic illness.

evidence.snippet: Classic galactosemia, which can result in life-threatening complications including feeding problems, failure to thrive, hepatocellular damage, bleeding, and E coli sepsis in untreated infants.

nodes.description: Altered signaling pathways are an additional downstream mechanism implicated in galactosemia beyond Gal-1-P accumulation alone.

evidence.snippet: Other currently described contributing factors include accumulation of galactose metabolites, uridine diphosphate (UDP)-hexose alterations and subsequent impaired glycosylation, endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress, altered signalling pathways, and oxidative stress.

nodes.description: Calcium, vitamin K1, and vitamin D3 supplementation is used to help prevent decreased bone mineralization during long-term management.
nodes.id: Calcium, vitamin K1, and vitamin D3 supplementation

evidence.snippet: Supplementation of calcium, vitamins K(1) and D(3) given in this dose (750 mg, 1.0 mg and 10.0 mug respectively) is likely to have a role in the treatment of BMC abnormalities in galactosemia.
Recommended calcium, vitamin D, and vitamin K intake to help prevent decreased bone mineralization

nodes.description: Oil-droplet cataracts from galactitol accumulation in the lens. May be present at birth or develop in first weeks of life.

evidence.snippet: Any single deficient enzyme can result in cataract through the accumulation of galactitol in the lens.

nodes.description: Classic galactosemia commonly affects brain function despite early dietary galactose restriction, producing cognitive, neuropsychological, neurologic, speech, and neuroimaging abnormalities in a variable long-term course.

evidence.snippet: Problems with cognitive function, neuropsychological/social emotional difficulties, neurological symptoms, and abnormalities in neuroimaging and electrophysiological assessments are frequently reported in this group of patients, with an enormous individual variability.

nodes.description: Increased susceptibility to gram-negative sepsis, particularly E. coli, in the neonatal period. Mechanism involves impaired neutrophil function and complement activation.

evidence.snippet: If the preliminary evaluation indicates galactosemia, there is high risk for E. coli sepsis and death.

nodes.description: Endoplasmic reticulum stress is an additional downstream mechanism implicated in galactosemia beyond Gal-1-P accumulation alone.

evidence.snippet: Other currently described contributing factors include accumulation of galactose metabolites, uridine diphosphate (UDP)-hexose alterations and subsequent impaired glycosylation, endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress, altered signalling pathways, and oxidative stress.
In classic galactosemia, erythrocyte galactose-1-phosphate is usually >10 mg/dL and erythrocyte GALT enzyme activity is absent or barely detectable.
The gold standard for diagnosis of classical galactosaemia is measurement of GALT activity in erythrocytes.

readouts.interpretation: Absent or barely detectable erythrocyte GALT activity directly reports the GALT enzymatic deficiency.
readouts.target: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

evidence.snippet: In classic galactosemia, erythrocyte galactose-1-phosphate is usually >10 mg/dL and erythrocyte GALT enzyme activity is absent or barely detectable.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated erythrocyte Gal-1-P reports the core intracellular Gal-1-P accumulation caused by the Leloir-pathway block.

nodes.description: Poor weight gain and growth failure in affected neonates.

evidence.snippet: Galactosemia in newborns and infants is associated with the following symptoms: jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, feeding difficulties, hypoglycemia, convulsions, lethargy, amino-aciduria, cataracts, hepatic cirrhosis, ascites, and mental retardation.

nodes.description: GALE encodes UDP-galactose-4-epimerase, which interconverts UDP-glucose and UDP-galactose. Deficient GALE activity causes epimerase-deficiency galactosemia with elevated galactose-1-phosphate in blood.

evidence.snippet: PURPOSE: UDP-galactose-4-epimerase (GALE) deficiency galactosemia is an autosomal recessive disorder and the prevalence of the disease varies among ethnic groups.
Galactosemia is a disorder caused by a deficiency of any one of three possible enzymes involved in the metabolism of galactose: galactokinase, transferase or epimerase.

nodes.description: GALK1 encodes galactokinase, which phosphorylates galactose to galactose-1-phosphate. Deficient galactokinase activity causes a galactosemia subtype in which excess galactose is diverted to galactitol, producing lens toxicity.

evidence.snippet: Galactosemia is a disorder caused by a deficiency of any one of three possible enzymes involved in the metabolism of galactose: galactokinase, transferase or epimerase.
Galactosemia is a disorder caused by a deficiency of any one of three possible enzymes involved in the metabolism of galactose: galactokinase, transferase or epimerase.

nodes.description: In classic galactosemia, deficient galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase activity disrupts the core uridylyltransferase step of galactose metabolism.
nodes.id: GALT Deficiency in the Leloir Pathway

evidence.snippet: Type I (classic) galactosemia, galactose 1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (GALT)-deficiency is a hereditary disorder of galactose metabolism.
The most prevalent mutation, Q188R, had a significant effect of genotype category (Q188R/Q188R, Q188R/Other, Other/Other) on POF

nodes.description: Excess galactose can be reduced to galactitol, which accumulates in the lens and causes osmotic injury. This is the dominant mechanism in galactokinase deficiency and also contributes to cataract risk in galactosemia more broadly.

evidence.snippet: Any single deficient enzyme can result in cataract through the accumulation of galactitol in the lens.

nodes.description: Galactose-1-phosphate accumulates to high intracellular levels in classic galactosemia and is an important contributor to downstream metabolic toxicity, but not the sole pathogenic factor.

evidence.snippet: One of the main alterations caused by GALT deficiency is the accumulation of galactose 1-phosphate (Gal-1P) in cells.
Results revealed that Gal-1-P is not the sole pathophysiological agent responsible for the phenotype observed in galactosemia.

nodes.description: Lifelong dietary restriction of galactose is the cornerstone of treatment. Lactose-containing foods (milk, dairy) are eliminated and replaced with soy-based or elemental formulas in infancy.

evidence.snippet: more than 80% and perhaps more than 90% are affected despite neonatal diagnosis and careful lifelong dietary restriction of galactose

nodes.description: Enlarged liver from hepatocellular damage and fatty infiltration.

evidence.snippet: Galactosemia in newborns and infants is associated with the following symptoms: jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, feeding difficulties, hypoglycemia, convulsions, lethargy, amino-aciduria, cataracts, hepatic cirrhosis, ascites, and mental retardation.

nodes.description: Estrogen and progesterone replacement for females with premature ovarian insufficiency.

evidence.snippet: Hormone replacement therapy as needed for delayed pubertal development and/or primary or secondary amenorrhea.
This means that in addition to the consequences of the disease, these women also face infertility and the potential need for hormone replacement therapy.

nodes.description: Abnormal glycosylation is a downstream consequence of disturbed UDP-hexose homeostasis and is thought to contribute to persistent neurologic and ovarian complications.

evidence.snippet: There is circumstantial evidence that defective galactosylation of complex glycoconjugates exists in tissues from galactosemic patients.
Other currently described contributing factors include accumulation of galactose metabolites, uridine diphosphate (UDP)-hexose alterations and subsequent impaired glycosylation

nodes.description: Cognitive impairment may occur despite early dietary treatment, likely due to endogenous galactose production and UDP-galactose deficiency affecting brain development.

evidence.snippet: Galactosemia in newborns and infants is associated with the following symptoms: jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, feeding difficulties, hypoglycemia, convulsions, lethargy, amino-aciduria, cataracts, hepatic cirrhosis, ascites, and mental retardation.

nodes.description: Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia appearing in the first week of life due to hepatocellular dysfunction.

evidence.snippet: Galactosemia in newborns and infants is associated with the following symptoms: jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, feeding difficulties, hypoglycemia, convulsions, lethargy, amino-aciduria, cataracts, hepatic cirrhosis, ascites, and mental retardation.

nodes.description: Oxidative stress is an additional downstream mechanism implicated in galactosemia beyond Gal-1-P accumulation alone.

evidence.snippet: Other currently described contributing factors include accumulation of galactose metabolites, uridine diphosphate (UDP)-hexose alterations and subsequent impaired glycosylation, endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress, altered signalling pathways, and oxidative stress.

nodes.description: Over 80% of females with classic galactosemia develop hypergonadotropic hypogonadism with primary or secondary amenorrhea.

evidence.snippet: Primary or premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is the most common long-term complication experienced by girls and women with classic galactosemia; more than 80% and perhaps more than 90% are affected despite neonatal diagnosis and careful lifelong dietary restriction of galactose.

nodes.description: Diminished bone mineral content has been reported in children and adolescents with classical galactosemia and is a target for calcium, vitamin K1, and vitamin D3 supplementation.

evidence.snippet: Classical galactosemia is an inherited disorder of galactose metabolism. Recently, diminished bone mineral content (BMC) in children and adolescents has been found.

nodes.description: Verbal dyspraxia and speech delays are common long-term complications even with early treatment.

evidence.snippet: Impairment of speech affects a significant number of patients with galactosemia, appears in early childhood, and persists into adulthood.

nodes.description: Early intervention for speech and language delays.

evidence.snippet: The BBC appears to be a viable intervention to support the speech and expressive language development of children with GC.

nodes.description: Movement disorders including tremor and ataxia may develop as long-term neurological complications.

evidence.snippet: Thirty-one patients showed evidence of motor dysfunction including: tremor (23 patients), dystonia (23 patients), cerebellar signs (6 patients), and pyramidal signs (4 patients).

nodes.description: GALT deficiency and Gal-1-P accumulation reduce intracellular UDP-hexose pools, especially UDP-galactose and related sugar donors.

evidence.snippet: intracellular concentrations of Gal-1-P found in classic galactosemia inhibit UDP-hexose pyrophosphorylases and reduce the intracellular concentrations of UDP-hexoses
Gas-chromatographic determination of urinary sugars and sugar alcohols demonstrates elevated concentrations of galactose and galactitol.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated urinary galactitol reports diversion of galactose to galactitol accumulation.
